title: The Viticulture Guide
subtitle: Grape growing and vineyard management from dormancy to harvest

overview.title: Understanding Viticulture
overview.description: Viticulture is the science, art, and practice of grape cultivation. While winemaking transforms grapes into wine, viticulture determines the raw material's quality. Great wine begins in the vineyard, and no amount of winemaking skill can compensate for poor fruit. Understanding how vines grow, what threatens them, and how farmers shape their development is essential to understanding wine itself.

old_world.title: Old World Approach
old_world.description: Terroir-driven viticulture emphasizes minimal intervention, allowing the site to express itself. Yields are controlled by tradition and appellation law. The vigneron is a steward of the land, not its master.

new_world.title: New World Approach
new_world.description: Science-driven viticulture emphasizes measurement, optimization, and adaptation. Sites are chosen and modified to suit grape varieties. The viticulturist uses technology to achieve consistent quality.

philosophy.convergence: Modern viticulture increasingly blends both approaches. Old World regions adopt precision tools; New World producers embrace lower-intervention farming. The goal remains the same: healthy vines producing balanced fruit.

key_terminology.definition: The vine's vegetative growth energy. High-vigor vines produce excessive shoots and leaves; low-vigor vines may struggle to ripen fruit. Balance is the goal.
The above-ground portion of the vine: shoots, leaves, and fruit. Canopy management shapes this growth for optimal sun exposure and air circulation.
A genetic variant of a grape variety, selected for specific characteristics like disease resistance, cluster size, or flavor profile.
The root system onto which the fruiting variety is grafted. Rootstocks provide phylloxera resistance and influence vigor, drought tolerance, and mineral uptake.
The amount of fruit produced, typically measured in tons per acre or hectoliters per hectare. Lower yields generally concentrate flavors.
The maturity of tannins, anthocyanins, and flavor compounds in grape skins and seeds, independent of sugar ripeness.
The temperature difference between day and night. Large shifts preserve acidity while allowing daytime ripening.
Controlled drought conditions that limit vegetative growth and concentrate fruit character. Excessive stress damages vines.

lifecycle.title: The Vine Lifecycle
lifecycle.description: The grapevine follows an annual cycle driven by temperature and day length. Understanding each stage helps predict harvest timing, anticipate problems, and appreciate how weather affects vintage quality. The cycle begins with winter dormancy and culminates in harvest, typically 100-120 days after flowering.

stages.name: Dormancy
stages.description: The vine enters a state of suspended animation as temperatures drop below 10C (50F). Sap retreats to the roots, leaves fall, and the vine appears dead. This period of rest is essential for the vine's long-term health and prepares it for the coming growing season.
physiological_changes: Carbohydrate reserves stored in roots and trunk
Cold hardiness develops (down to -15C for V. vinifera)
vineyard_activities: Soil work and cover crop planting
risks: Extreme cold (below -20C) can kill buds or vines
Warm spells can break dormancy prematurely
Crown gall infection at pruning wounds
stages.impact_on_wine: The pruning decisions made during dormancy determine the next vintage's potential yield and fruit quality. Retaining more buds increases yield but may dilute quality.

regional_notes.burgundy: Pruning typically December-March, governed by tradition
regional_notes.napa: Mild winters; pruning can start earlier

stages.name: Budbreak
stages.description: As soil temperatures reach 10C (50F), dormant buds swell and burst, revealing tiny shoots and the first leaves. This marks the beginning of the growing season and the point of maximum frost vulnerability.
visual_signs: Green shoot tips emerge from buds
Sap begins to flow (visible at pruning cuts)
vineyard_activities: First disease sprays (sulfur for powdery mildew)
risks: Spring frost (most damaging at this stage)
Uneven budbreak from poor pruning or vine stress
stages.impact_on_wine: The timing and uniformity of budbreak sets the trajectory for the vintage. Early budbreak risks frost; late budbreak may not allow full ripening in cool climates.

stages.name: Shoot Growth
stages.description: Shoots grow rapidly, sometimes 2-3 cm per day in warm conditions. Leaves expand and begin photosynthesis. The vine's energy is focused on vegetative growth, building the canopy that will ripen the fruit.
risks: Excessive vigor produces too much canopy
stages.impact_on_wine: Canopy management during this period determines fruit exposure and air circulation. Excessive shading produces vegetal flavors; excessive exposure causes sunburn.
stages.vigor_management: High-vigor sites may need multiple shoot thinning passes. Low-vigor sites benefit from reduced competition (cover crop mowing, irrigation).

stages.name: Flowering
stages.description: Tiny flowers open and self-pollinate. This brief, critical period determines how many berries will set on each cluster. Weather during flowering dramatically affects yield and cluster uniformity.
risks: Rain during flowering causes coulure (poor fruit set)
Hot, dry conditions can cause shatter
stages.impact_on_wine: Poor flowering reduces yield but often improves quality (fewer, more concentrated berries). Millerandage (uneven berry size) from partial set affects ripening uniformity.

coulure_millerandage.coulure: Flower or berry drop from poor fertilization. Reduces yield, often improves quality.
coulure_millerandage.millerandage: Uneven berry size from partial fertilization. Small berries (hens) with large berries (chicks). Can be positive for complexity.

stages.name: Fruit Set
stages.description: Fertilized flowers develop into small, hard, green berries. The number of berries per cluster is now fixed, determining potential yield. Unfertilized flowers and weak berries drop from the cluster.
visual_signs: Small green berries visible (size of peppercorns)
stages.impact_on_wine: Berry count per cluster is now fixed. This determines potential yield and berry concentration. Fewer berries generally means more concentrated wine.
stages.typical_set_rates: Normal fruit set ranges from 25-50% of flowers. Higher isn't always better; moderate set produces better quality.

stages.name: Veraison
stages.duration: 1-2 weeks for color change to complete
stages.description: The onset of ripening, marked by color change in red grapes (green to purple) and softening/translucence in white grapes. This is the most critical inflection point in the growing season, when the vine shifts from growth mode to ripening mode.
visual_signs: Red varieties: berries turn from green to red/purple
White varieties: berries turn from green to golden/translucent
physiological_changes: Sugar accumulation accelerates (0.5-1 Brix per day)
Berry size doubles from fruit set
stages.impact_on_wine: The timing and uniformity of veraison affects final wine quality. Uneven veraison results in flavor inconsistency. The 50-60 days from veraison to harvest are when most flavor compounds develop.
stages.countdown_to_harvest: Typical: 45-60 days from veraison to harvest. Cooler climates: longer; warmer climates: shorter.

stages.name: Ripening
stages.description: The final development phase where sugars accumulate, acids decline, and flavor compounds reach optimal levels. The winemaker's most important decisions are made now: when to pick, whether to wait for more ripeness, how to balance sugar and acid.
visual_signs: Seeds turn from green to brown
vineyard_activities: Regular sampling for sugar, acid, pH
risks: Heat spikes can cause sunburn or raisining
Overripeness (sugar too high, acid too low)
stages.impact_on_wine: Every day during ripening changes the potential wine. Earlier picking preserves acidity and freshness; later picking increases alcohol and richness. The decision is the most important of the vintage.

ripeness_monitoring.phenolic: Seed color, tannin taste, flavor development

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: The culmination of the growing season. Grapes are picked at optimal ripeness and transported to the winery. Timing, method, and logistics all affect final wine quality. This is the most labor-intensive and exciting time in wine regions.

harvest_decisions.when: Balance of sugar, acid, and phenolic ripeness

stages.impact_on_wine: Harvest decisions are irreversible. Picking too early produces green, acidic wine; too late produces flabby, alcoholic wine. The target depends on wine style.

hand.best_for: Premium wines, steep slopes, uneven ripening

machine.best_for: Large volume, flat terrain, uniform ripening

training_systems.title: Training Systems
training_systems.description: Training systems are the physical structures that shape vine growth. Each system balances fruit exposure, disease pressure, labor requirements, and mechanization. The choice of training system depends on climate, variety, site, and tradition. No system is universally best; each represents a set of tradeoffs.

systems.name: Vertical Shoot Positioning (VSP)
systems.origin: Europe, refined in Germany and New World
systems.description: Shoots are trained vertically between pairs of catch wires, creating a narrow, hedge-like canopy. The fruit zone is exposed below the foliage, allowing good air circulation and spray penetration.
systems.climate_suitability: Cool to moderate (struggles in hot climates)
characteristics: Clean fruit zone with good air flow
disadvantages: Higher trellis costs than head training

systems.name: Gobelet (Bush Vine)
systems.description: Free-standing vines without trellising, shaped like a goblet or cup. Multiple arms radiate from a short trunk, each bearing fruit. This ancient system requires no infrastructure and is extremely drought-resistant.
disadvantages: Difficult to manage in fertile soils

systems.name: Guyot Simple
systems.description: One fruiting cane is trained along the wire, with a renewal spur for next year's cane. This system limits production naturally and is standard in Burgundy and many premium regions.

systems.name: Guyot Double
systems.description: Two fruiting canes trained in opposite directions, each with its own renewal spur. Doubles the production potential of single Guyot while maintaining quality.

structure.renewal_spurs: Two, at base of each cane

characteristics: Good balance of quantity and quality

systems.name: Cordon de Royat
systems.description: A permanent horizontal arm (cordon) bears evenly spaced spurs. Each spur produces one or two shoots with fruit. This simple system is easily mechanized and requires less skill than cane pruning.
disadvantages: May need cordon renewal over time

systems.name: Bilateral Cordon
systems.description: Two permanent arms extend in opposite directions from the trunk, each bearing spurs. This maximizes production while maintaining simplicity.
disadvantages: Can compromise quality at high yields

systems.name: Pergola (Tendone)
systems.description: Vines are trained overhead on a horizontal or sloping canopy. Fruit hangs below, shaded by leaves above. Traditional in hot climates where shade is beneficial.

systems.name: Geneva Double Curtain (GDC)
systems.description: Two separate downward-growing curtains of foliage, divided by a gap. Designed to manage excessive vigor by spreading the canopy over a larger area.
advantages: Improves fruit quality in vigorous sites
Better disease control vs single curtain

systems.name: Lyre (U-shaped)
systems.description: A U-shaped canopy with two vertical sides, resembling a lyre. The open center allows excellent light penetration while managing vigor.

systems.name: Smart-Dyson
systems.description: A divided canopy with shoots trained both upward and downward, creating a vertical 'ballerina' effect. Designed for high vigor and hot climates.

structure.division: Upper shoots up, lower shoots down

systems.name: Scott Henry
systems.description: A modified VSP with two fruiting zones: upper shoots trained up, lower shoots trained down. Effectively doubles the fruiting area while maintaining quality.

canopy_management.title: Canopy Management
canopy_management.description: Canopy management encompasses all the techniques used to manipulate vine foliage and fruit exposure. The goal is to optimize photosynthesis while ensuring fruit receives appropriate sunlight and air circulation. Poor canopy management leads to disease, uneven ripening, and vegetal flavors.

concepts.explanation: Leaves need light to photosynthesize. Interior leaves in dense canopies become inefficient and eventually parasitic.
Fruit needs some sun for color development and disease prevention, but excessive exposure causes sunburn.
Good airflow dries moisture and reduces fungal disease pressure.
Adequate leaves are needed to ripen fruit. Typically 15-20 leaves per shoot for balance.

techniques.name: Shoot Thinning
techniques.description: Removing excessive shoots to reduce canopy density and improve remaining shoot vigor. Done early when shoots are easily removed by hand.
technique: Target 4-6 shoots per foot of cordon
techniques.purpose: Reduces canopy density, improves air circulation, concentrates vine energy
techniques.timing_critical: Yes - must be done early or wounds are larger and labor increases
techniques.impact_on_wine: Reduces vegetal character, improves fruit concentration, aids disease control

techniques.name: Shoot Positioning
techniques.timing: Late spring through early summer (multiple passes)
techniques.description: Training shoots into the trellis wires to create an organized canopy. Essential for VSP and similar systems.
techniques.purpose: Creates uniform canopy, exposes fruit zone, enables mechanization
techniques.timing_critical: Yes - easier when shoots are flexible
techniques.impact_on_wine: Uniform ripening, consistent quality, reduces disease

techniques.name: Leaf Pulling
techniques.description: Removing leaves around grape clusters to improve air circulation, spray penetration, and fruit exposure. One of the most impactful canopy operations.
technique: Remove leaves in fruit zone (1-3 leaves per shoot)
Focus on shaded side first (east in morning sun regions)
Avoid excessive exposure in hot climates
Can be done by hand or machine
techniques.purpose: Disease prevention, color development, flavor ripening
techniques.timing_critical: Yes - too early risks sunburn; too late limits benefit

climate_considerations.cool: More aggressive leaf pulling for ripeness and disease
climate_considerations.hot: Minimal on sun-exposed side to prevent sunburn

techniques.impact_on_wine: Reduces green/vegetal flavors, improves color extraction, enhances aromatic development

techniques.name: Hedging (Topping)
techniques.timing: Throughout growing season as shoots exceed trellis
techniques.description: Mechanically trimming shoot tips that extend beyond the trellis. Maintains tidy canopy and redirects energy to fruit.
technique: Cut shoot tips extending above/beyond trellis
techniques.purpose: Maintains canopy shape, reduces shading, redirects energy
techniques.timing_critical: Not critical - can be done as needed
techniques.controversy: Some believe excessive hedging reduces photosynthetic capacity and stresses vines
techniques.impact_on_wine: Maintains vine balance, can increase fruit concentration

techniques.name: Lateral Removal
techniques.description: Removing secondary shoots (laterals) that grow from leaf axils. These consume energy without contributing much photosynthesis.
technique: Remove lateral shoots in fruit zone
Can leave upper laterals for photosynthesis
techniques.purpose: Opens canopy, focuses vine energy, improves spray coverage
techniques.timing_critical: No - can be done throughout season
techniques.impact_on_wine: Improves fruit quality through better exposure

techniques.name: Cluster Zone Management
techniques.description: Holistic management of the area where fruit hangs, combining leaf pulling, lateral removal, and positioning to optimize conditions.
technique: Adjust based on variety and climate
techniques.purpose: Optimal fruit development, disease prevention, even ripening

goals.light: Dappled to partial sun on clusters

techniques.impact_on_wine: Consistent ripening, optimal flavor development, healthy fruit

pests_diseases.title: Vineyard Pests & Diseases
pests_diseases.description: Grapevines face constant threats from fungi, insects, viruses, and environmental stresses. Understanding these threats is essential for producing healthy fruit. Integrated Pest Management (IPM) combines cultural, biological, and chemical controls to minimize damage while reducing environmental impact.

fungal.name: Powdery Mildew
fungal.description: The most widespread fungal disease of grapevines worldwide. Unlike downy mildew, powdery mildew thrives in dry conditions and doesn't require moisture for infection.
fungal.origin: Native to North America; devastated Europe in 1850s
fungal.impact_on_wine: Severe infections impart musty/moldy off-flavors. Even 3-5% infected fruit can be detected.

fungal.name: Downy Mildew
fungal.description: A devastating oomycete disease requiring moisture for infection. Can defoliate vines rapidly in wet conditions.
fungal.origin: Native to North America; arrived in Europe 1878

organic: Good for prevention, less for cure

fungal.impact_on_wine: Infected fruit is unusable. Severe infections cause defoliation, preventing ripening.

fungal.name: Botrytis
fungal.description: A fungus that can be devastating (gray rot) or beneficial (noble rot) depending on conditions. Noble rot concentrates sugars for great dessert wines.

noble_rot.conditions: Morning moisture, afternoon drying, late season

prevention: Leaf and lateral removal in fruit zone
fungal.impact_on_wine: Gray rot ruins wine. Noble rot creates some of the world's greatest dessert wines.

fungal.name: Esca (Black Measles)
fungal.description: A complex of trunk diseases that has become increasingly devastating worldwide. Kills vines over years or suddenly (apoplexy). No cure exists.
fungal.treatment: No effective cure. Trunk renewal or vine removal only options.
fungal.impact_on_wine: Gradual decline reduces fruit quality. Sudden death loses entire vines.

fungal.name: Black Rot
fungal.description: A fungal disease that mummifies berries. Particularly severe in humid continental climates like the Eastern US.
fungal.conditions_favoring: Warm, wet weather during early fruit development
fungal.treatment: Mancozeb, myclobutanil, or other fungicides before infection

insects.name: Phylloxera
insects.description: The root-feeding aphid that devastated European vineyards in the 1860s-1880s. Controlled by grafting onto resistant American rootstocks. See the Rootstock Guide for full history.
insects.current_status: Managed through grafting; own-rooted V. vinifera remains vulnerable

insects.name: Grape Berry Moth
insects.description: Caterpillars bore into berries, causing rot and crop loss. Multiple generations per year in warm climates.
insects.impact_on_wine: Damaged berries rot and must be sorted out

insects.name: Mealybugs
insects.description: Sap-sucking insects that excrete honeydew, promoting sooty mold. More importantly, they vector grapevine leafroll virus.
symptoms: White, waxy insects on bark and clusters
insects.concern: Primary vector for grapevine leafroll virus (GLRaV)

insects.name: Leafhoppers
insects.description: Sap-sucking insects that cause leaf stippling. The glassy-winged sharpshooter vectors Pierce's disease in California.

special_concern.name: Glassy-winged Sharpshooter
special_concern.impact: Can kill vines; major threat in California

insects.control: Usually not economically damaging; treat only for severe infestations or sharpshooter presence

viruses.name: Grapevine Leafroll Virus
viruses.description: The most economically important virus complex affecting grapevines. Multiple strains exist (GLRaV-1, -2, -3, etc.). Reduces yield and delays ripening.

viruses.name: Grapevine Red Blotch Virus
viruses.description: Recently identified virus (2012) that reduces fruit quality. Distinct from leafroll despite similar symptoms.
viruses.discovery: First identified in California in 2012; now found worldwide
viruses.impact: Reduces sugar accumulation, delays ripening, diminishes wine quality
viruses.control: Certified clean material; roguing; research ongoing

environmental.name: Frost Damage
environmental.description: Freezing temperatures during the growing season can destroy entire crops. Spring frost at budbreak is most damaging; winter cold can kill vines entirely.

spring_frost.result: Burned shoots, total crop loss possible

winter_freeze.result: Bud death, cane death, trunk death

protection_methods.how: Mix warm air from inversion layer down to vines
Ice releases heat as it forms, protecting tissue

environmental.name: Hail Damage
environmental.description: Hailstones can shred leaves, damage shoots, and bruise or destroy fruit. Damage is immediate and unrepairable. Some regions are particularly hail-prone.

environmental.name: Drought Stress
environmental.description: Water deficit affects vine growth, fruit development, and wine quality. Moderate stress is often beneficial; severe stress damages vines and prevents ripening.

environmental.name: Sunburn
environmental.description: Excessive sun exposure damages grape skins, causing browning and off-flavors. Increasingly common with climate change and aggressive leaf removal.
environmental.conditions: Sudden exposure (aggressive leaf pulling), heat waves, reflective surfaces
prevention: Maintain some shade on west/south exposure

harvest.title: Harvest Decisions
harvest.description: Harvest is the culmination of the vintage. The decision of when to pick balances sugar accumulation, acid preservation, and phenolic ripeness. There is no perfect formula - the target depends on wine style, variety, and conditions.

metrics.name: Brix (Sugar Level)
metrics.considerations: High Brix doesn't mean quality. Over-ripe fruit can lack freshness and balance.

metrics.name: pH
metrics.significance: Indicates acid strength, affects microbial stability, color, aging
metrics.considerations: Low pH = stable, fresh wine. High pH = risk of microbial problems, flat taste.

metrics.name: Titratable Acidity (TA)
metrics.significance: Total acid content - affects taste, freshness, balance
metrics.considerations: TA decreases during ripening as malic acid is metabolized. Hot nights speed acid loss.

metrics.name: Phenolic Ripeness
metrics.significance: Maturity of skins and seeds - affects tannin quality and flavor

indicators.skin_texture: Difficult to chew = unripe; easily mashed = ripe

metrics.considerations: Phenolic ripeness may lag behind or exceed sugar ripeness. Balance is key.

metrics.name: Flavor/Aroma Development
assessment: Walk the vineyard and taste berries
Note fruit character: green, fresh fruit, ripe fruit, overripe
metrics.considerations: Experience matters. Learn to correlate taste with final wine character.

methods.name: Hand Harvest
methods.description: Workers pick clusters by hand using knives or shears. The traditional method for premium wine.

methods.name: Machine Harvest
methods.description: Mechanical harvesters straddle vine rows, shaking berries free with rods. Collected on conveyor belts.
disadvantages: Can damage vines if not careful
methods.improvements: Modern machines with optical sorting reduce MOG significantly

methods.name: Night Harvest
methods.description: Picking at night or early morning when temperatures are coolest. Common in warm climates.
advantages: Better working conditions in hot climates

factors.consideration: Sparkling = early; Late harvest = very late
Rain coming? Pick early. Heat wave? Wait or pick early.
Different blocks ripen at different times

practices.title: Farming Practices
practices.description: How a vineyard is farmed reflects philosophy, economics, and environmental concerns. From conventional to biodynamic, each approach has advocates and critics. The trend is toward reduced-input farming, though definitions vary.

approaches.name: Conventional Viticulture
approaches.philosophy: Use the most effective tools available to produce healthy crops. Synthetic inputs are tools, not inherently bad.
approaches.market_position: Still majority of global production, declining percentage

approaches.name: Sustainable Viticulture
approaches.philosophy: Reduce inputs and environmental impact through Integrated Pest Management (IPM) and best practices. Pragmatic environmentalism.

inputs.approach: IPM - spray only when necessary, use softest effective option

approaches.market_position: Growing rapidly, especially in New World

approaches.name: Organic Viticulture
approaches.philosophy: Eliminate synthetic chemicals entirely. Work with natural systems. Trust that nature, properly managed, provides balance.
disadvantages: Higher disease risk in wet years
approaches.copper_debate: Copper is toxic to soil organisms and accumulates. Organic farming's reliance on copper is controversial.
approaches.market_position: Growing steadily; ~10% of global vineyard area in conversion or certified

approaches.name: Biodynamic Viticulture
approaches.philosophy: The vineyard is a self-contained organism connected to cosmic rhythms. Strengthen the vine's life forces through specific preparations and timing.

preparations.name: Horn Manure
preparations.purpose: Stimulate soil life and root growth

calendar.description: Activities timed to lunar and planetary positions

core_practices.farm_organism: Vineyard should be self-sufficient with livestock, composting, biodiversity

approaches.scientific_view: Studies show biodynamic farms have good soil health, but attribute this to overall attentive farming rather than specific preparations.

approaches.name: Regenerative Viticulture
approaches.philosophy: Beyond sustainability - actively improve soil health and sequester carbon. Leave the land better than you found it.

vine_age.title: Vine Age & Vieilles Vignes
vine_age.description: Vine age affects wine character. Young vines produce vigorous growth but dilute fruit; old vines produce less but more concentrated fruit. 'Old vine' claims are largely unregulated, making the term both meaningful and potentially misleading.

stages.name: Young Vines
stages.fruit_usage: Usually declassified or discarded. Not used for premium wine.

stages.name: Productive Vines
stages.fruit_usage: Standard production. Quality improving with age.

stages.name: Old Vines
stages.fruit_usage: Top cuvees. Often labeled as 'Old Vine' or equivalent.

stages.name: Ancient Vines
stages.fruit_usage: Most prestigious wines. Heritage value beyond fruit.

factors.explanation: Older vines have deeper roots, accessing water and minerals younger vines cannot reach
Young vines put energy into growth; old vines focus on fruit
Old vines naturally produce less, more concentrated fruit
Decades of selection pressure favor well-adapted vines
Old vine root systems host complex fungal networks that aid nutrient uptake

regulation.title: The Labeling Problem
regulation.content: Most wine regions have no legal definition of 'old vine.' A wine labeled 'Vieilles Vignes' could be from 25-year-old or 100-year-old vines. Some voluntary standards exist:
regulation.advice: Ask producers the actual vine age if claims seem dubious. Reputable producers are transparent.

famous_old_vines.note: Among oldest continuously producing vines in world

yield_management.title: Yield Management
yield_management.description: Yield - the amount of fruit produced per vine or hectare - is a key quality lever. Lower yields generally mean more concentrated fruit, but the relationship is not linear. Yields must balance economic reality with quality goals.

units.metric: Hectoliters per hectare (hl/ha) or Tons per hectare (t/ha)

measurement.conversion: 1 ton/acre ≈ 17.5 hl/ha (varies by variety and juiciness)

techniques.name: Green Harvest
techniques.description: Removing entire clusters of unripe (green) fruit to reduce crop load and concentrate ripening energy in remaining fruit.
technique: Remove second crop (clusters from lateral shoots)
disadvantages: May increase sugar too much if overdone
techniques.cost_benefit: Expensive operation. Usually reserved for premium wines where concentration justifies cost.

techniques.name: Cluster Thinning
techniques.description: Removing clusters earlier than green harvest, before significant resources have been invested in the fruit.
technique: Remove to target (e.g., one cluster per shoot)

techniques.name: Pruning for Yield
techniques.description: The number of buds retained during pruning sets maximum yield potential. Fewer buds = lower yield.
techniques.balance: Must match vine capacity. Under-pruning over-produces; over-pruning stresses vines.

appellation_regulations.title: Maximum Yields by Region
appellation_regulations.note: Many appellations set maximum permitted yields. These limit quantity to theoretically ensure quality.
appellation_regulations.controversy: Critics argue maximum yields are set too high and rarely limit quality. Defenders note they prevent extreme overproduction.

yield_quality_relationship.title: Does Lower Yield Mean Higher Quality?
nuance: The relationship is not linear - halving yield doesn't double quality
Below certain thresholds, quality doesn't improve (vine is already balanced)
Variety matters - some varieties need crop to balance
Vine balance matters more than absolute yield numbers
Site potential is the ultimate limiter
yield_quality_relationship.guideline: Target the yield that achieves balance for your site and variety. This may be 30 hl/ha or 70 hl/ha depending on context.

climate_challenges.title: Climate Challenges
climate_challenges.description: Grapevines face an array of weather challenges that can damage crops or compromise quality. Climate change is intensifying many of these threats while creating new ones.

challenges.name: Climate Change
challenges.description: Rising temperatures, altered precipitation patterns, and increased extreme events are reshaping viticulture globally. Wine regions are migrating, varieties are shifting, and traditional practices are being reconsidered.
observed_changes: Earlier budbreak and harvest (2-3 weeks over 50 years)
Higher potential alcohol from increased sugars
Lower acidity from faster malic acid loss
Increased drought stress in some regions

adaptation_strategies.examples: Mourvedre instead of Grenache; Albarino in cooler sites
Moving from valley floor to hillsides
Pick at lower Brix, accept lower alcohol

migration.description: Wine regions are shifting poleward and to higher elevations
examples: England: Now producing quality sparkling wine

challenges.name: Wildfires & Smoke Taint
challenges.description: Wildfires are increasingly common in wine regions. Beyond direct destruction, smoke exposure during ripening causes smoke taint - off-flavors that render wine undrinkable.

smoke_taint.cause: Volatile phenols from smoke absorbed by grape skins
smoke_taint.testing: Lab analysis for free and bound smoke markers
smoke_taint.problem: Compounds bind to sugars, releasing during fermentation and aging

mitigation.winemaking: Minimal skin contact, activated charcoal (removes flavor)

challenges.reality: No good solution exists. Affected fruit is often abandoned.

challenges.name: Extreme Heat Events
challenges.description: Heat waves (>40C/104F) cause sunburn, raisining, shutdown of ripening, and vine stress. Increasing in frequency and intensity.
damage: Stuck ripening: Sugars rise but flavors don't develop
management: Maintain some leaf cover for shade
Harvest before or after extreme heat

challenges.name: Water Scarcity
challenges.description: Declining water availability from drought, aquifer depletion, and competing demands threatens vineyard viability in many regions.

dry_farming.requirements: Deep soils, winter rainfall, low-vigor sites
dry_farming.examples: Much of Europe, parts of California
dry_farming.quality_argument: Some argue dry-farmed fruit has superior concentration

famous_sites.title: Famous Vineyard Sites
famous_sites.description: What makes legendary vineyards special? Beyond soil and climate, viticulture practices shape these sites' reputations. Each represents generations of accumulated wisdom about how to farm that specific place.

sites.name: Romanee-Conti
sites.soil: Limestone with clay, iron-rich, shallow topsoil over Bathonian limestone
sites.what_makes_it_special: Perfect combination of site (limestone bedrock, slope, exposure), history (farmed since 12th century), and obsessive attention. The DRC team treats the vineyard as sacred ground.

sites.name: Hill of Grace
sites.soil: Sandy loam over clay, ancient pre-Cambrian soils
sites.what_makes_it_special: 160-year-old own-rooted Shiraz vines, possibly oldest in world. The vines themselves are living heritage, each with gnarly trunk 50cm+ diameter.

sites.name: To Kalon Vineyard
sites.soil: Deep alluvial gravel over clay, Bale loam
viticulture_notes: The name means 'the highest beauty' in Greek
sites.what_makes_it_special: The quintessential Napa Cabernet site. Deep gravel provides perfect drainage; benchland position offers warm days and cool nights. Multiple iconic wines share this soil.

sites.name: La Mouline
viticulture_notes: Workers tied to stakes on steep slopes
Mica reflects heat, schist retains it
sites.what_makes_it_special: Extreme viticulture on near-vertical slopes. The 'blonde' soils (lighter schist with mica) produce perfumed, elegant Syrah distinct from darker 'brune' slopes.

sites.name: Scharzhofberg
viticulture_notes: Slate retains heat on cool slopes
sites.what_makes_it_special: One of Germany's greatest vineyards. The steep slate slopes capture every ray of sun in this marginal climate, producing Rieslings of crystalline purity and profound minerality.

sites.name: Pisoni Vineyard
viticulture_notes: Extreme wind site - natural vigor control
sites.what_makes_it_special: Gary Pisoni proved California could produce Burgundian-style Pinot Noir through high-density planting and site selection. The 'Pisoni clone' is now planted throughout California.

sites.name: Les Clos
sites.what_makes_it_special: The largest and most famous Chablis Grand Cru. The ancient oyster-shell fossils in the soil contribute the mineral tension that defines great Chablis. Southwest exposure ensures full ripeness.

sites.name: Chateau Rayas / La Crau
viticulture_notes: Eccentric viticulture - almost no green harvest
sites.what_makes_it_special: The contrarian legend of Chateauneuf. While others celebrate galets, Rayas' sandy plot produces the most ethereal, Burgundian expression of Grenache through ultra-low yields and old vines.
